options.why: Took out $+2$; expanding $2(2x + 5) = 4x + 10$, the opposite sign of the original.
Did not flip the second sign; $-10 \div -2 = +5$, so it is $+5$ inside.
Flipped the constant's sign but forgot to divide it by $2$, leaving $10$ instead of $5$.
Left the constant negative; $-9 \div -1 = +9$, so it should be $+9$ inside.
Dropped the negative factor; $x + 9$ expands to $x + 9$, not $-x - 9$.
Treated $9$ as the common factor; $-9(x + 1) = -9x - 9$ changes the $x$ coefficient.
Did not flip the $+9$; dividing $9$ by $-3$ gives $-3$, not $+3$.
Took out $+3$ instead of $-3$; $3(2x - 3) = 6x - 9$ has both signs reversed.
Kept the $-12y$ term negative; $-12y \div -4y = +3$, so it is $+3$ inside.
Took out $+4y$; $4y(2y + 3) = 8y^2 + 12y$, the opposite sign of the original.
Left $y^2$ after dividing; $-8y^2 \div -4y = 2y$, dropping one power of $y$.
Took out $+4y$; every term then expands with the wrong sign.
Took out $-4$ only, leaving the common $y$ inside — not fully factorised.
Dropped the $z$ when dividing $-20xyz$ by $-4y$; the quotient is $5xz$.

questions.q: Factorise $-6 + 10p^2$ by taking out a negative common factor.

options.why: Did not flip the $p^2$ term; $10p^2 \div -2 = -5p^2$, so it is $-5p^2$ inside.
Took out $+2$; $2(3 - 5p^2) = 6 - 10p^2$, the opposite sign of every term.
Dropped the square; $10p^2 \div -2 = -5p^2$, which keeps the power.
